name: PA53 improved well-being, gut symptoms, sleep, and inflammatory biomarkers in older adults
summary: The PA53 trial adds a short-duration older-adult human signal for well-being, gut symptoms, sleep quality, inflammatory biomarkers, and microbiota composition.
statement: In healthy older adults aged 55 to 70 years, eight weeks of Pediococcus acidilactici PA53 supplementation improved happiness, gastrointestinal symptoms, stool-form score, sleep quality, serum inflammatory cytokines, and microbiota composition compared with baseline or placebo-relevant comparisons reported in the abstract.
population_or_model: Healthy older adults aged 55 to 70 years
quantitative_note: The trial enrolled 75 participants and used 30 billion CFU/day PA53. Reported PA53-group changes included MUNSH happiness 33.75 to 38.05, GSRS 17.50 to 15.83, Bristol stool scale 2.50 to 3.20, PSQI 5.62 to 4.33, IL-6 135.6 to 108.4 pg/mL, TNF-alpha 65.8 to 54.2 pg/mL, and IL-25 48.8 to 68.5 pg/mL.
caveats: The trial was short and enrolled healthy older adults rather than frail or diseased older adults.

The main functional endpoints were emotional well-being, gastrointestinal symptoms, stool form, and sleep quality rather than frailty, sarcopenia, intrinsic capacity, disease progression, or mortality.
The abstract does not list a ClinicalTrials.gov registration ID.
Microbiota and cytokine changes are supportive biomarkers and do not establish durable aging efficacy.
The source reports no author conflicts, but independent replication is still needed.
